planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in full sun to partial shade after last frost; million bells is vegetatively propagated (sterile hybrids do not come true from seed) and sold exclusively as rooted cuttings or potted plants.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in full sun to partial shade after last frost; million bells (superbells) are trailing, mounding hybrids derived from South American Calibrachoa species that produce thousands of tiny, one-inch, petunia-like flowers throughout the growing season in virtually every color — from pure white through yellow, pink, coral, red, purple, magenta, and bicolor combinations. They are among the top-selling container annuals in the US for their continuous bloom, compact trailing habit, and resistance to flopping and deadheading requirements (they are self-cleaning). Require consistently moist, well-fertilized growing media — plants in containers quickly exhaust the available nutrients and benefit from weekly liquid fertilizer at half-strength during active growth. Excellent in hanging baskets, window boxes, and as trailers over container edges.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil or potting mix in full sun to partial shade after last frost; fan flower tolerates coastal salt spray, heat, and humidity far better than most annuals, making it ideal for Gulf Coast container gardens.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil or potting mix in full sun to partial shade after last frost; fan flower is an Australian native that produces an almost continuous cascade of distinctive fan-shaped flowers in lavender-blue, purple, white, or pink — each flower has five petals arranged like an open hand or fan, all on one side (the upper half of the flower is missing, giving it a uniquely asymmetric appearance). It trails attractively from hanging baskets and container edges and tolerates heat, humidity, coastal conditions, and mild drought significantly better than most summer annuals. Self-cleaning (no deadheading required). One of the most heat-tolerant lavender-blue-flowered annuals for Gulf South summer gardens. 'Blue Wonder' and 'Surdiva Blue' are excellent long-blooming selections.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil or potting mix in full sun to partial shade after last frost; bidens blooms almost immediately from transplant and continues until frost.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; bidens (Apache beggartick, golden goddess) is a trailing, fine-textured annual from the arid Mexican highlands that produces a continuous, self-cleaning display of bright golden-yellow, five-petaled daisy-like flowers against the ferny, deeply divided foliage from late spring until frost. The fine texture and trailing habit make it ideal for softening container arrangements and hanging baskets, where it weaves through and around companion plants. Extremely heat-tolerant, drought-resistant once established, and nearly indestructible in containers. Occasionally self-sows in mild climates. 'Goldmarie', 'Peter's Gold Carpet', and 'Golden Goddess' are popular compact selections. Excellent as a combination trailer with salvia, vinca, and zinnias.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in partial shade in spring; ornamental bacopa performs best in cool weather (below 85°F) and often declines in peak summer heat, rebounding when temperatures moderate in fall.

directions: Plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile potting mix in partial shade; ornamental bacopa (not to be confused with the aquatic herb Bacopa monnieri) is a delicate, trailing South African plant with tiny, rounded, aromatic leaves and a continuous profusion of minuscule, five-petaled flowers in pure white, lavender-pink, or magenta that create a soft, frothy effect in hanging baskets, window boxes, and container edges throughout the spring and fall cool seasons. It is self-cleaning and requires no deadheading. Plants go into heat-induced dormancy in peak summer temperatures above 85°F — they may decline in midsummer heat but often recover in fall. Trim back heat-stressed plants by half to encourage recovery growth in cooler fall weather. Excellent with 'Proven Winners' combination container plants as a fine-textured filler.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; silver king artemisia spreads aggressively by underground rhizomes and should be sited where its spreading habit can be contained or accommodated.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; silver king artemisia is a native North American prairie and plains plant grown for its exceptionally bright, woolly-silver, aromatic foliage that is among the most useful and effective silver foliage plants in the temperate garden — the upright, wand-like stems and broadly lance-shaped silver-white leaves create a luminous, light-gathering effect in borders that is especially effective in the evening. The plant is extremely drought-tolerant once established and thrives in hot, dry, alkaline conditions. It spreads by underground rhizomes to form large colonies; divide every 2–3 years in spring to maintain vigor and control spread. Excellent as a foil for richly colored flowers and for cutting and drying. 'Silver Queen' (broader leaves, more compact) and 'Valerie Finnis' (bright silver, outstanding form) are fine named selections.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
